steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a textual evidence question asking which critic's quotation most directly challenges the underlined claim. The claim is that Medellín's style is "so idiosyncratic that it resists efforts to view his work through the lens of nationality or cultural identity." To challenge it, a quotation must show the work actually invites classification by nationality or cultural identity.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Although a number of ancient Indigenous artistic traditions pictured human forms in profile, the forms populating the surface of A History of Mexico suggest a specifically Maya influence.": Identifying one influence does not argue the whole work invites cultural classification or resists the "idiosyncratic" label. It supports, rather than challenges, the idea that many traditions blend in the work. ✗
  • Choice B — "In A History of Mexico, the synthesis of ancient and modernist traditions functions as a stylistic parallel to the work's subject matter: a survey of centuries of Mexican history.": Describing how multiple traditions fuse into a unified style actually reinforces the claim that the work defies single-category classification rather than challenging it. ✗
  • Choice C — "Many critics focus on Indigenous influences in A History of Mexico and other key works by Medellín to the exclusion of influences from non-Indigenous art.": This argues critics should view all influences equally, which supports viewing the work as idiosyncratic and resisting narrow cultural lenses — it does not challenge the underlined claim. ✗
  • Choice D — "While A History of Mexico features modernist motifs, it relies primarily on angular human forms in profile—a staple of Maya sculpture—and thus invites classification as Indigenous art.": Directly states the work "invites classification as Indigenous art," the exact cultural identity framing the claim says the work resists. This is a head-on challenge. ✓
D is correct.
